notes: multitude of slain, and a great number of carcases; and [there is] none end of [their] corpses; they stumble upon their corpses:
No (Hebrew No Amon, believed by many scholars to be Thebes which was destroyed in approximately 667 B.C.).

quotes.text: Apocalyptic visions are highly symbolic, usually requiring an angelic interpreter for the seer to understand what he sees. The symbols are inclusive-that is, they stand for categories into which all specific instances of something can be placed. This is why the great and abominable church can be called Nineveh (some of John's language comes from the description of Nineveh in Nahum 3)
quotes.speaker: Stephen E. Robinson
quotes.attr: Stephen E. Robinson, "Warring against the Saints of God," Ensign, Jan 1988, 36

notes: Habakkuk asked the question of the people of Judah as to why God would use the wicked and proud Chaldean nation as His agent for Judah's punishment. Habakkuk was surely aware of King Josiah's death (609 B. C. ), Egypt's domination of Judah in 609-605 B.C., and the victory of the Chaldean army over Egypt at the Battle of Charchemish (605 B. C. ). After this last mentioned battle, the Chaldeans controlled the lion's share of the earth Messages or Central Themes The Voice..., pp. 368-375. 1. Habakkuk's first question is one of universal and timeless application. The sense of that question might be stated: Why does evil and wickedness continue and seem to prevail over the righteous? Why doesn't God do something about it? This is the question of theodicy. Habakkuk's plea was full of anguish: O Lord, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear ! even cry out unto thee of violence, and thou wilt not save! Why dost thou shew me iniquity, and cause me to behold grievance? for spoiling and violence are before me: and there are that raise up strife and contention. Therefore the law is slacked, and judgment cloth never go forth: for the wicked doth compass about the righteous; therefore wrong judgment proceedeth. (1:2-4) The Lord's answer to Habakkuk's questions about violence and iniquity and injustice was that he was going to punish the wicked. Men will be punished according to their works. But Jehovah informed Habakkuk that he was going to use the great Chaldean (Babylonian) nation as his agent for this punishment (1:5-11). Babylon shall sweep upon Judah and take it captive. But the Babylonians will believe it was their own god and their own power that made them great. They will become proud and will not recognize that Jehovah is the only true God of the whole earth and so they, too, in time will be destroyed and punished. All the prophets taught that Jehovah was the universal God over the earth, as did Habakkuk. It is also almost unanimous in the teachings of the prophets that God does direct the affairs of nations, and has his hand in the direction of history.

In a strict sense, any attempt to solve the seeming injustices and evils that the righteous suffer is called a "theodicy." The study of Job and Habakkuk are theodicies. They attempt to solve questions such as: Why does God allow the wicked to prosper ? Why do the righteous suffer? Why does God create a world and allow evil and suffering in it? The prophet Habakkuk voiced the anguish of many souls who suffered oppression, injustices, and lawlessness which prevailed during the reign of Jehoiakim (609-598 B. C.). This king brought back conditions as they had been in the days of the wicked King Manasseh.

HABAKKUK While this book is true prophecy, its method is quite different from other writings of the prophets. Dramatically constructed in the form of dialogue, this book contains the prophet’s complaints (questions) and God’s reply to them. In god’s answers Habakkuk discovers the doorway leading from questioning to affirmation, through which he enters into a faith that enables him to affirm, "I will rejoice in the Lord… God, the Lord, is my strength."

For Habakkuk, it was the answer to his questions. It was the solution to apparent evils and misfortunes and sufferings that the righteous endure. No matter what happens in this world which we cannot understand--things in this world which seem inconsistent with the power and goodness of God--by having faith we provide a keel on our ship which will not let the vessel overturn, even in the most terrible tempest.
